title_en: Taharah: Al-Kasani's Conditions and Pillars Framework
content_en:
The full title's emphasis on 'arranging the divine laws' is most evident in the taharah sections of Bada'i as-Sana'i, where al-Kasani's systematic framework — conditions (shurut), pillars (arkan), invalidating factors (nawaqid) — organizes Hanafi purification law with unusual clarity.

For the validity of wudu', al-Kasani separates the pre-conditions (shurut) from the act's own obligatory components. The conditions for valid wudu' include: the water must be pure and purifying (tahir mutahhir); the person must remove any barrier (hajiz) that prevents water from reaching the skin — including water-resistant substances like wax or heavy grease; and the wudu' must be performed in the correct circumstances. These pre-conditions must be met before the wudu' even begins.

The pillars of wudu' (its obligatory elements that constitute the act) are the four Quranic fara'id: washing the face, washing the arms, wiping the head (minimum one quarter), and washing the feet. Al-Kasani explains why these four are the pillars: they are established by the definitive text of the Quran (5:6) and their omission renders the wudu' entirely void — unlike the wajib acts (such as maintaining the sequence, beginning with the right side, and the Basmala) whose omission is blameworthy but does not void the wudu'.

The invalidating factors of wudu' (nawaqid) are those acts that break the wudu' once it has been validly performed. Al-Kasani lists them: discharge from the front or back passages, loss of consciousness (including sleep, fainting, and intoxication), ghusl-triggering conditions, touching one's private parts with the inner surface of the hand (in the position that this constitutes a hadath), and apostasy. He explains why each of these breaks the wudu' with reference to the evidential basis in the hadith and the reasoning of the school's imams.

For ghusl, the same framework applies. The conditions include the availability of pure water and the removal of barriers. The pillars of ghusl are: intention, rinsing the mouth (as part of the body cavity's accessible opening), rinsing the nostrils, and washing the entire body. The invalidating factors are the ghusl-triggering conditions themselves reversed — once ghusl has been performed and the person exits the state of major impurity, the factors that would require a new ghusl are: sexual intercourse, ejaculation, and the end of menstruation or postpartum bleeding.

This conditions-pillars-invalidating factors framework, consistently applied throughout Bada'i as-Sana'i, gives students of Hanafi law a reliable analytical structure. When confronting a novel question in taharah, they can ask: Is there a missing condition? Is there a missing pillar? Has an invalidating factor occurred? Al-Kasani's systematic presentation makes this diagnostic process natural and reliable.

The taharah chapters of Bada'i as-Sana'i demonstrate the work's enduring value: it is not merely a collection of rulings but an organized system that students can internalize and apply, making it one of the most pedagogically effective works in the Hanafi tradition.
title_id: Thaharah: Kerangka Syarat dan Rukun Al-Kasani
content_id:
Penekanan judul lengkap pada 'menyusun hukum-hukum ilahi' paling tampak dalam bagian-bagian thaharah dari Bada'i as-Sana'i, di mana kerangka sistematis al-Kasani — syarat-syarat (syuruth), rukun-rukun (arkan), faktor-faktor pembatal (nawaqidh) — mengorganisasi hukum thaharah Hanafi dengan kejelasan yang tidak biasa. Untuk setiap jenis thaharah — wudhu, mandi wajib, tayamum, menghilangkan najis — al-Kasani terlebih dahulu mendefinisikan konsep, menyatakan syarat-syarat sahnya, mengidentifikasi rukun-rukun esensialnya, dan mendaftar apa yang membatalkannya. Hasilnya adalah uraian thaharah yang tidak ada bandingnya dalam hal kemudahan akses dan kejelasan presentasi.

Al-Kasani memberikan perhatian khusus pada pertanyaan-pertanyaan di mana posisi madzhab Hanafi berbeda dari madzhab-madzhab lain, dan beliau mempresentasikan dasar Hanafi untuk posisi-posisi tersebut dengan cara yang memperjelas mengapa madzhab mengambil pandangannya. Diskusinya tentang air dan kategori-kategorinya, tentang kondisi-kondisi di mana najis membatalkan thaharah, dan tentang keadaan-keadaan khusus yang memengaruhi status ritual seseorang bersifat luar biasa menyeluruh dan telah menjadi teks referensi standar untuk hukum thaharah Hanafi.

Analisis al-Kasani tentang thaharah sangat berguna bagi siswa karena ekspresivitas pedagogisnya: beliau tidak hanya menyatakan posisi madzhab tetapi menjelaskan struktur analitis yang menghasilkan posisi-posisi tersebut. Membaca Bada'i as-Sana'i pada thaharah memberikan pemahaman tentang cara para ulama Hanafi berpikir tentang masalah-masalah hukum, bukan hanya kesimpulan-kesimpulan yang mereka capai.
content_ar: The full title's emphasis on 'arranging the divine laws' is most evident in the الطهارة sections of Bada'i as-Sana'i, where al-Kasani's systematic framework — conditions (shurut), pillars (arkan), invalidating factors (nawaqid) — organizes الحنفي purification law with unusual clarity. For the validity of الوضوء', al-Kasani separates the pre-conditions (shurut) from the act's own obligatory components. The conditions for valid الوضوء' include: the water must be pure and purifying (tahir mutahhir); the person must remove any barrier (hajiz) that prevents water from reaching the skin — بما في ذلك water-resistant substances like wax or heavy grease; and the الوضوء' must be performed in the correct circumstances. These pre-conditions must be met قبل the الوضوء' even begins. The pillars of الوضوء' (its obligatory elements that constitute the act) are the four القرآني fara'id: washing the face, washing the arms, wiping the head (minimum one quarter), and washing the feet. Al-Kasani explains why these four are the pillars: they are established by the definitive text of the القرآن الكريم (5:6) and their omission renders the الوضوء' entirely void — unlike the الواجب acts (مثل maintaining the sequence, beginning with the الحق side, and the Basmala) whose omission is blameworthy but does not void the الوضوء'. The invalidating factors of الوضوء' (nawaqid) are those acts that break the الوضوء' حين it has been validly performed. Al-Kasani lists them: discharge from the front or back passages, loss of consciousness (بما في ذلك sleep, fainting, and intoxication), الغسل-triggering conditions, touching one's private parts with the inner surface of the hand (in the position that this constitutes a hadath), and apostasy. He explains why each of these breaks the الوضوء' with reference to the evidential basis in the الحديث and the reasoning of the school's imams. For الغسل, the same framework applies. The conditions include the availability of pure water and the removal of barriers. The pillars of الغسل are: intention, rinsing the mouth (as part of the الجسد cavity's accessible opening), rinsing the nostrils, and washing the entire الجسد. The invalidating factors are the الغسل-triggering conditions themselves reversed — حين الغسل has been performed and the person exits the state of major impurity, the factors that would require a new الغسل are: sexual intercourse, ejaculation, and the end of menstruation or postpartum bleeding. This conditions-pillars-invalidating factors framework, consistently applied throughout Bada'i as-Sana'i, gives students of الحنفي law a reliable analytical structure. عندما confronting a novel question in الطهارة, they can ask: Is there a missing condition? Is there a missing pillar? Has an invalidating factor occurred? Al-Kasani's systematic presentation makes this diagnostic process natural and reliable. The الطهارة الأبواب of Bada'i as-Sana'i demonstrate the work's enduring value: it is not merely a collection of ruling
